era_context: Egypt's Fourth Dynasty is in its final years or has recently concluded; the Fifth Dynasty is beginning or about to begin. Mesopotamia is in the Early Dynastic Period III, with city-states competing for dominance. The Indus Valley Civilization is in its mature phase. China's Xia Dynasty is either legendary or in its earliest stages with no contemporary records.

events.title: Reign of Shepseskaf, Fourth Dynasty Egypt (if extended to this year)
events.description: Shepseskaf, the last pharaoh of the Fourth Dynasty, may still be reigning in this year, or his reign may have recently ended. His reign is poorly documented compared to his predecessors. He is credited with completing the pyramid of Menkaure and constructing a mastaba (bench-shaped tomb) rather than a pyramid for himself, possibly signaling a shift in royal ideology. His reign is brief (traditional estimate: c. 2487–2480 BCE), and the reasons for the transition to the Fifth Dynasty are unclear.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Shepseskaf's mastaba at Saqqara

events.certainty_note: Shepseskaf's reign is poorly attested. Traditional dating places him c. 2487–2480 BCE, but this is uncertain by ±20–50 years. His assignment to this specific year is conventional.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization mature phase continues
events.region: Indus Valley (modern Pakistan and northwest India)
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization remains in its mature phase with no documented major changes or disruptions in this year. Urban centers continue to function, trade networks remain active, and cultural practices show continuity. Archaeological evidence does not reveal significant events or transitions in this specific year.

sources.name: Archaeological stratigraphy from Indus Valley sites

events.certainty_note: The Indus Valley Civilization's continued existence is confirmed archaeologically, though individual years cannot be distinguished.

disconfirming_evidence: Shepseskaf's reign is one of the least well-documented periods of the Old Kingdom. Some scholars question whether he was truly the last Fourth Dynasty pharaoh or whether the dynastic division is a later historiographic construct. The significance of his choice to build a mastaba rather than a pyramid is debated—it may reflect religious change, economic constraints, or simply personal preference. No contemporary records explain the transition to the Fifth Dynasty.
historiographic_note: For 2477 BCE, the historical record is extremely sparse. Egyptian king lists provide names and regnal lengths, but these are compiled centuries or millennia after the events and contain systematic uncertainties. No contemporary written records exist for most of the world. The assignment of events to specific years in this period is largely conventional; the archaeological record typically allows only ±50–100 year precision.

graph_edges.note: Shepseskaf's reign ends; Userkaf and the Fifth Dynasty begin
